options.A: It must be transformed, typically by a 1x1 convolution with the matching stride and channel count, so the shapes agree
options.B: It must be replaced by a concatenation
options.C: The block must be split into two blocks
options.D: Nothing, addition broadcasts automatically

questions.explanation: The addition requires identical shapes on both branches, so the shortcut needs a projection whenever the residual path changes the resolution or the depth.

questions.question: A convolutional network ends with global average pooling before its classifier rather than a flatten. What is the practical advantage?

options.A: Each feature map collapses to one value, so the classifier's input size no longer depends on the spatial size of the image
options.B: It removes the need for an activation function
options.C: It increases the number of features available to the classifier
options.D: It makes the network trainable without backpropagation

questions.explanation: A flatten ties the classifier's input dimension to the input resolution, so the network only accepts one image size. Global average pooling removes that constraint and also cuts a large number of parameters.

questions.question: You have a small labelled dataset of defect images and access to a network pre-trained on a large natural-image dataset. What is the standard approach?

options.A: Average the weights of several randomly initialised networks
options.B: Train from scratch, since the two domains differ
options.C: Use the pre-trained network without any training and accept its original classes
options.D: Reuse the pre-trained weights and fine-tune on the new task, since early features such as edges transfer

questions.explanation: Transfer learning is most valuable exactly in this situation: the new dataset is too small to learn good low-level features, but those features are largely task independent.

questions.question: Your grayscale images must be fed to a network whose first convolution expects three input channels. What is the simplest correct fix?

options.A: Repeat the single channel three times to form an RGB-shaped input
options.B: Feed the image three times in sequence
options.C: Change the loss function
options.D: Pad the tensor with two channels of zeros

questions.explanation: Replicating the channel keeps the image's appearance intact for filters that expect natural-image statistics, whereas zero channels would present the network with a strongly coloured input it never saw during pre-training.

questions.question: What is one epoch of training?

options.A: One complete pass over the training set
options.B: One parameter update
options.C: One forward pass through the network
options.D: One evaluation of the validation set

questions.explanation: An epoch consists of many updates - one per mini-batch - so the number of updates per epoch is the dataset size divided by the batch size.

questions.question: Why is data augmentation applied to the training set but not to the validation set?

options.A: The validation score should measure model quality, not vary with random transforms
options.B: Augmentation is too slow for validation
options.C: The validation set has no labels
options.D: Augmentation only works on large datasets

questions.explanation: A validation score that changes on every evaluation cannot be compared across epochs, which would make early stopping and model selection unreliable.

questions.question: A dataset stores labels in a separate file that maps each image filename to a class index. What must a data generator do with them?

options.A: Convert them to floating point images
options.B: Shuffle them independently of the images
options.C: Return them alongside the corresponding images, in the same order as the batch
options.D: Normalise them to zero mean and unit variance

questions.explanation: Shuffling labels independently of the images would break the correspondence entirely, which is a classic source of a model that never learns.

questions.question: Which of these layers has no trainable parameters?

options.B: ReLU
options.C: Convolutional
options.D: Fully connected

questions.explanation: ReLU is a fixed element-wise function. Batch normalization does have trainable parameters, its scale and shift, in addition to the statistics it tracks.

questions.question: In which order are layers executed during the forward and backward passes?

options.A: Trainable layers first, then the rest
options.B: Forward from input to loss, backward from loss to input
options.C: Both passes run from loss to input
options.D: Both passes run from input to loss

questions.explanation: The backward pass must reverse the forward order, because each layer's gradient depends on the gradient of the layer that consumed its output.

questions.question: What does a plain stochastic gradient descent update do to a weight?

options.A: Multiplies it by the learning rate
options.B: Subtracts the gradient scaled by the learning rate
options.C: Adds the gradient scaled by the learning rate
options.D: Replaces it with the gradient

questions.explanation: The gradient points in the direction of steepest increase, so minimising the loss means stepping against it.

questions.question: What is the output of a cross-entropy loss for one mini-batch?

options.A: A matrix the same shape as the predictions
options.B: One value per sample
options.C: One value per class
options.D: A single scalar summarising the whole batch

questions.explanation: Gradient descent needs a scalar objective, so the per-sample losses are summed or averaged before the backward pass begins.

questions.question: What is the output of ReLU for the input vector (-2, 0, 3)?
questions.explanation: ReLU passes non-negative values unchanged and clamps everything negative to zero.

questions.question: What does the softmax function guarantee about its output vector?

options.A: All entries are non-negative and sum to one
options.B: All entries lie between -1 and 1
options.C: The entries are sorted in descending order
options.D: Exactly one entry is 1 and the rest are 0

questions.explanation: That is precisely what allows the output to be interpreted as a probability distribution over the classes.

questions.question: Why must the weights of a layer be initialised randomly rather than all to the same value?

options.A: To speed up the forward pass
options.B: To reduce the number of parameters
options.C: To break symmetry, so that units in a layer can learn different features
options.D: To make training reproducible

questions.explanation: Identical weights give identical gradients, so the units would remain copies of one another for the whole of training.

questions.question: What quantity does an optimizer need in addition to the current weights?

options.B: The validation accuracy
options.C: The gradient of the loss with respect to those weights
options.D: The network architecture

questions.explanation: This separation is what allows the same optimizer to be used with any layer type, since it only sees weights and gradients.

questions.question: A pooling layer with a 2x2 window and stride 2 is applied to a 32x32 feature map. What is the output size?
questions.explanation: A stride of 2 halves each spatial dimension, which is the usual way a convolutional stack reduces resolution as depth increases.

questions.question: How many channels does a max-pooling layer produce from an input with 64 channels?
questions.explanation: Pooling operates within each channel independently, so only the spatial dimensions change.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a weight initialisation scheme such as Xavier or He?

options.A: To reduce the number of parameters
options.B: To choose the learning rate automatically
options.C: To keep the scale of the activations and gradients stable across layers at the start of training
options.D: To decide how many layers the network needs

questions.explanation: A poor initial scale makes the signal either vanish or explode as it propagates, so training stalls before it begins.

questions.question: Which optimizer maintains both a first and a second moment estimate of the gradient?

options.B: Adam
options.C: SGD with momentum
options.D: Gradient clipping

questions.explanation: Momentum keeps only the first moment. Adam adds the second, which is what gives it a per-parameter step size.

questions.question: What is the effect of 'same' padding with stride 1?

options.A: The number of channels is preserved
options.B: The output has the same spatial size as the input
options.C: The kernel size is halved
options.D: The output is smaller than the input

questions.explanation: Zero padding replaces the border pixels the kernel would otherwise lose, so layers can be stacked without the feature maps shrinking away.

questions.question: What does the regularization weight, often called alpha or lambda, control?

options.B: The batch size
options.C: How strongly the penalty term influences the total loss relative to the data term
options.D: The fraction of units dropped

questions.explanation: Too large and the model underfits because the penalty dominates; too small and it has no regularizing effect at all.

questions.question: Which statement about dropout is correct?

options.A: It normalises the activations of a layer
options.B: It reduces the learning rate over time
options.C: It removes units permanently from the network
options.D: It deactivates a random subset of units on each training iteration

questions.explanation: A different random subset is drawn each iteration, so over training the network is effectively an ensemble of many thinned sub-networks.

questions.question: What does batch normalization normalise?

options.A: The activations, using statistics computed over the mini-batch
options.B: The gradients
options.C: The weights of the layer
options.D: The learning rate

questions.explanation: It then applies a learned scale and shift, so the network can undo the normalisation if that turns out to be better.

questions.question: What does the hidden state of a recurrent cell represent?

options.B: The network's parameters
options.C: A summary of the sequence seen so far, passed to the next time step
options.D: The gradient at the current step

questions.explanation: It is the only route by which information from earlier inputs reaches later outputs.

questions.question: Which activation functions are typically used inside an LSTM's gates?

options.B: ReLU and softmax
options.C: Sigmoid and tanh
options.D: Linear and softmax

questions.explanation: A gate must output a value between 0 and 1 so it can act as a soft switch, which is exactly the range of the sigmoid.

questions.question: What is a residual or skip connection?

options.A: A connection that skips the loss function
options.B: A connection that adds a block's input to its output
options.C: A connection between two different networks
options.D: A connection that removes a layer during training

questions.explanation: It gives the gradient a short path back through the network and lets a block represent the identity when its residual branch is near zero.

questions.question: Why is a model switched to evaluation mode before inference?

options.B: So that layers such as dropout and batch normalization use their inference behaviour
options.C: To free memory
options.D: To reset the weights

questions.explanation: Forgetting this is a common bug: dropout stays active and the batch statistics keep updating, so predictions become noisy and non-deterministic.

questions.question: What does the F1 score combine?

options.B: Accuracy and loss
options.C: Precision and recall
options.D: Training and validation error

questions.explanation: It is their harmonic mean, so it is only high when both are high - a model that finds every defect by flagging everything scores poorly.

questions.question: Which output activation suits a multi-label problem where several labels can be active at once?

options.B: An independent sigmoid per label
options.C: A single ReLU
options.D: No activation at all

questions.explanation: Softmax forces the outputs to compete for a fixed total, which is exactly wrong when two labels can be true simultaneously.

questions.question: What does global average pooling produce from a feature map with C channels?

options.A: A vector of length C, one average per channel
options.B: A feature map of half the spatial size
options.C: A vector of length equal to the number of pixels
options.D: A single scalar

questions.explanation: Because the spatial extent collapses entirely, the following classifier no longer depends on the input resolution.

questions.question: Your training loss decreases steadily but your validation loss starts rising after epoch 12. What does this indicate and what is the standard response?

options.A: Overfitting from epoch 12 onwards; stop early and keep the model from around that point
options.B: Underfitting; train for longer
options.C: The learning rate is too small; increase it
options.D: The validation set is mislabelled

questions.explanation: The divergence between the two curves is the classic overfitting signature, and the epoch where the validation curve turns is exactly what early stopping is designed to catch.

questions.question: You train a network and the loss does not change at all from the first iteration. Which cause is most plausible?

options.A: The model has too many layers
options.B: The validation set is too small
options.C: The learning rate is zero, or the gradients are not reaching the parameters
options.D: The batch size is too large

questions.explanation: A completely flat loss points at a broken update path - a zeroed learning rate, gradients never computed, or an optimizer that was never given the parameters - rather than at a modelling problem.

questions.question: What does a single neuron compute before its activation is applied?

options.B: A weighted sum of the inputs plus a bias
options.C: The distance to the nearest class centre
options.D: The product of all inputs

questions.explanation: Everything else in a layer is built on this one affine operation.

questions.question: You have 200 labelled images and a set of features that already work well. What should you try first?

options.A: A network trained without validation data
options.B: A classical model on those features
options.C: A deep network trained from scratch
options.D: A deeper network with no regularisation

questions.explanation: 200 samples is far too few to learn a representation. Fine-tuning a pretrained network would be the other sensible option.

questions.question: Your model scores 99% on training data and 62% on validation data. What is happening?

options.B: The validation set is too big
options.C: The learning rate is too small
options.D: It is overfitting

questions.explanation: The fix is more data, augmentation or regularisation, not more capacity.

questions.question: What does model capacity refer to?

options.B: The size of the training set
options.C: The range of functions it can fit
options.D: The memory the model needs

questions.explanation: Capacity grows with width and depth and sits at the heart of the bias-variance trade-off.

questions.question: Why did the XOR result damage early neural network research?

options.A: A limit of one linear layer was read as a limit of the whole idea
options.B: It showed convolution was required
options.C: It proved backpropagation was wrong
options.D: It showed networks can never approximate functions

questions.explanation: One hidden layer solves XOR. The result was correct but got over-generalised.

questions.question: What separates supervised from unsupervised learning?

options.B: Unsupervised learning cannot be measured
options.C: Unsupervised learning needs more compute
options.D: Supervised learning trains against given labels

questions.explanation: Unsupervised objectives still exist, they just come from the data rather than from labels.

questions.question: All your class A photos were shot outdoors and all class B photos indoors. Why is that a problem?

options.B: Lighting separates the classes, so the model can learn that instead
options.C: The images have different sizes
options.D: It is fine if the dataset is large

questions.explanation: The model takes whichever shortcut is easiest, and that shortcut breaks as soon as the correlation does.

questions.question: A model underfits badly. Which change is most likely to help?

options.B: Increase the model capacity
options.C: Shrink the training set
options.D: Add more weight decay

questions.explanation: Dropout and weight decay both fight overfitting, so they would make underfitting worse.

questions.question: Why is a training, validation and test split used rather than just training and test?

options.A: So tuning decisions do not contaminate the final score
options.B: To make training faster
options.C: To reduce the parameter count
options.D: Because test sets are usually mislabelled

questions.explanation: Once you tune on a split, its score is no longer an honest estimate.

questions.question: What does backpropagation compute?

options.A: Gradients of the loss for every parameter
options.B: The updated weights
options.C: The forward activations
options.D: The best learning rate

questions.explanation: Applying those gradients is the optimizer's job, which is why any optimizer can be swapped in.

questions.question: You stack two fully connected layers with nothing between them. What have you built?

options.B: A model with twice the capacity
options.C: A model that cannot be trained
options.D: Something equivalent to one fully connected layer

questions.explanation: Two affine maps compose into one. Depth only pays off with a non-linearity in between.

questions.question: Why does each layer learn a bias as well as weights?

options.A: It lets the boundary miss the origin
options.B: It normalises the inputs
options.C: It stops weights growing
options.D: It keeps activations positive

questions.explanation: Without a bias every layer is pinned to the origin, which is a real restriction.

questions.question: You set every weight in a layer to the same non-zero value. What happens?

options.B: The units stay identical and never differentiate
options.C: Training is slower but works
options.D: The biases break the tie

questions.explanation: Identical weights give identical gradients, so the whole layer behaves like one unit.

questions.question: A fully connected layer maps 100 inputs to 50 outputs. How many parameters, biases included?
questions.explanation: 100 x 50 weights plus one bias per output gives 5050.

questions.question: What does the universal approximation theorem actually promise?

options.B: One hidden layer can approximate any continuous function
options.C: Deeper is always better
options.D: Gradient descent finds the global optimum

questions.explanation: It is an existence result only. It says nothing about how wide the layer must be or whether training will find it.

questions.question: Given that one hidden layer is enough in theory, why build deep networks?

options.A: Depth reuses features, so fewer units are needed
options.B: Deep networks never overfit
options.C: Shallow networks cannot use ReLU
options.D: Deep networks have convex losses

questions.explanation: The width a shallow network would need is often impractical. Depth expresses the same thing far more compactly.

questions.question: You feed a uniform grey image into a trained classifier. Are all class scores equal?

options.A: Yes, the input has no class information
options.B: Only for a batch size of one
options.C: No, each class has different weights
options.D: Yes, if the output is a softmax

questions.explanation: Equal scores would need identical weight vectors for every class, which training never produces.

questions.question: Your loss turns into NaN partway through training. What is the first thing to suspect?

options.B: The validation set is too large
options.C: The learning rate is too high
options.D: The batch size is too small

questions.explanation: Lower the rate, clip the gradients, and check for a log of zero in the loss.

questions.question: Which quantity does a fully connected layer have to keep from its forward pass?

options.B: Its own output
options.C: Its input
options.D: The learning rate

questions.explanation: The weight gradient is built from the stored input and the incoming error.

questions.question: In what order do the forward and backward passes run?

options.B: Trainable layers first
options.C: Both loss to input
options.D: Forward input to loss, backward loss to input

questions.explanation: Each layer's gradient depends on the one above it, so the backward pass must reverse the order.

questions.question: Adding more layers to a plain network makes the training error go up. What does that suggest?

options.B: An optimisation problem, not a generalisation one
options.C: The batch size is too large
options.D: The labels are wrong

questions.explanation: Overfitting would lower the training error. This is the degradation problem that motivated ResNet.

questions.question: How does a gradient descent step change a weight?

options.B: Replaces it with the gradient
options.C: Multiplies it by the gradient
options.D: Subtracts the scaled gradient

questions.explanation: The gradient points uphill, so minimising means stepping against it.

questions.question: What does the learning rate control?

options.B: How many samples each update uses
options.C: How many layers get updated
options.D: How far each step moves

questions.explanation: Too large and it overshoots, too small and training crawls.

questions.question: Three loss curves are very noisy, mildly noisy and smooth. Which method produced the smooth one?

options.B: Full-batch gradient descent
options.C: Single-sample SGD
options.D: Mini-batch with batch size 2

questions.explanation: Averaging over more samples lowers the variance of the gradient estimate.

questions.question: Which loss goes with a softmax output for multi-class problems?

options.B: Mean absolute error
options.C: Hinge loss
options.D: Cross entropy

questions.explanation: Cross entropy is the negative log-likelihood of the right class under the softmax.

questions.question: Which loss suits a regression task with roughly Gaussian noise?

options.B: Squared error
options.C: Cross entropy
options.D: Binary cross entropy

questions.explanation: Squared error is the maximum likelihood choice under Gaussian noise.

questions.question: Your binary classifier ends with ReLU, then sigmoid, and thresholds at 0.5. Is this sensible?

options.B: No, nothing can score below 0.5
options.C: Yes, if you raise the threshold
options.D: Yes, the sigmoid bounds the output

questions.explanation: ReLU output is never negative and sigmoid(0) is 0.5, so every sample comes out positive.

questions.question: Why decay the learning rate as training goes on?

options.B: It stops gradients vanishing
options.C: Gradients grow over time
options.D: A big step cannot settle into a minimum

questions.explanation: The step that made early progress is too coarse near the end, so the loss just bounces around.

questions.question: What does Adam add over plain momentum?

options.B: A larger learning rate
options.C: A second moment, giving per-parameter step sizes
options.D: Weight decay

questions.explanation: Dividing by the typical gradient size makes each parameter's step roughly scale-free.

questions.question: Why does Adam apply bias correction?

options.A: The learning rate is too high early on
options.B: The moment estimates start at zero and read too low
options.C: The bias terms are otherwise ignored
options.D: The gradients can be negative

questions.explanation: A running average starting from zero underestimates until enough terms build up.

questions.question: Would an activation that is flat below -0.5, linear in between, and flat above 0.5 train well?

options.A: Yes, it is a bounded ReLU
options.B: No, it is discontinuous
options.C: No, units in the flat parts get no gradient
options.D: Yes, bounded outputs help

questions.explanation: It is continuous, so that is not the issue. The flat regions kill the learning signal.

questions.question: You feed a GAN sorted batches: all dogs, then all cats. Why is that bad?

options.A: Each batch gradient is biased towards one class
options.B: The discriminator cannot handle two classes
options.C: The batches are too large
options.D: It is fine within one epoch

questions.explanation: Mini-batch methods assume each batch represents the data, and sorted batches break that.

questions.question: What does a loss function return for one mini-batch?

options.B: One value per sample
options.C: A single number
options.D: One value per class

questions.explanation: Gradient descent needs a scalar, so per-sample losses are summed or averaged first.

questions.question: Why is ReLU usually preferred to sigmoid in hidden layers?

options.A: Its gradient is 1 on the positive side
options.B: It has trainable parameters
options.C: It is bounded, so activations cannot blow up
options.D: It is easier to differentiate

questions.explanation: The sigmoid derivative peaks at 0.25 and nearly vanishes in its tails, so gradients shrink layer by layer.

questions.question: What problem does leaky ReLU fix?

options.B: Overfitting
options.C: Units that stop learning once their input stays negative
options.D: Slow convergence on the positive side

questions.explanation: A small negative slope keeps a gradient alive so the unit can recover.

questions.question: Why does a convolutional layer need far fewer weights than a fully connected one?

options.A: It only sees part of the image
options.B: It ignores colour channels
options.C: It uses fewer bits per weight
options.D: One kernel is reused at every position

questions.explanation: Weight sharing also encodes the idea that a useful pattern is worth spotting anywhere.

questions.question: Which claim about a valid convolution is false?

options.B: It uses no padding
options.C: Its output is smaller than same padding gives
options.D: It keeps the input size

questions.explanation: That describes same padding. Valid convolution shrinks each dimension by k-1.

questions.question: What is the receptive field of a unit after two stacked 3x3 convolutions, stride 1?
questions.explanation: Each layer widens it by 2. Two 3x3 layers beat one 5x5 on parameter count and add a non-linearity.

questions.question: What is a 1x1 convolution good for?

options.B: Widening the receptive field
options.C: Mixing channels and changing their number cheaply
options.D: Raising the spatial resolution

questions.explanation: It is a per-pixel linear map across channels, often used to cut depth before an expensive layer.

questions.question: What does a dilated convolution buy you?

options.B: Faster training
options.C: A wider receptive field at no extra parameter cost
options.D: Guaranteed translation invariance

questions.explanation: Gaps between the kernel taps widen the field fast with depth, which is why segmentation models like it.

questions.question: What does max pooling have to remember for its backward pass?

options.B: Nothing, it has no parameters
options.C: The whole input
options.D: The window averages

questions.explanation: Only the winning element affected the output, so only it receives gradient.

questions.question: What does a flatten layer do between convolutions and a dense layer?

options.A: Reshapes the feature maps into a vector
options.B: Averages each channel to one value
options.C: Normalises the activations
options.D: Cuts the channel count

questions.explanation: Averaging per channel is global average pooling, a different thing. Flatten keeps every value.

questions.question: Why is a stride above 1 sometimes called subsampling?

options.B: It averages nearby pixels
options.C: It cuts the channel count
options.D: It only evaluates the kernel every s positions

questions.explanation: That is why a strided convolution can be done as stride 1 followed by dropping rows and columns.

questions.question: Why does an even kernel need uneven padding to keep the input size?

options.B: It has no centre pixel to balance around
options.C: The stride must also be even
options.D: It does not, padding is the same

questions.explanation: An odd kernel pads (k-1)/2 on each side. For an even one no integer works on both sides.

questions.question: What is regularisation for?

options.B: Removing the validation set
options.C: Speeding up convergence
options.D: Shrinking the model file

questions.explanation: It trades a little training accuracy for a smaller gap to unseen data.

questions.question: A weight histogram shows a tall spike exactly at zero. Which regulariser was used?

options.B: L2
options.C: Dropout
options.D: None

questions.explanation: L2 shrinks weights towards zero without reaching it. L1 has constant gradient, so it arrives.

questions.question: Your model overfits and you suspect many input features are useless. Which regulariser first?

options.B: L2, since it shrinks everything
options.C: L1, since it zeroes irrelevant weights
options.D: Removing the biases

questions.explanation: L2 would shrink those weights but leave them contributing noise.

questions.question: What is dropout actually attacking?

options.B: Vanishing gradients
options.C: Class imbalance
options.D: Units that only work alongside particular partners

questions.explanation: If any partner might vanish, the layer has to learn more redundant features.

questions.question: What does dropout do at inference time?

options.B: Drops units but doubles the outputs
options.C: Drops the same fraction as in training
options.D: Nothing, the full network is used

questions.explanation: Predictions need to be deterministic, which is why the scaling correction happens during training.

questions.question: Inverted dropout divides surviving activations by the keep probability during training. Why?

options.A: It makes the activations larger, so training converges sooner
options.B: It removes the need to store the mask used in the forward pass
options.C: It keeps the expected activation the same, so testing needs no rescaling
options.D: It lets gradients bypass the layer during backpropagation

questions.explanation: The correction has to happen somewhere. Doing it in training leaves inference clean.

questions.question: Why can batch normalisation not use batch statistics at test time?

options.B: A prediction would depend on the other samples in the batch
options.C: It would be too slow
options.D: Batches are too small then

questions.explanation: The same image could get different answers depending on what it was batched with, so stored running estimates are used.

questions.question: In convolutional batch normalisation, what are the statistics computed over?

options.B: Each pixel position separately
options.C: The whole tensor at once
options.D: The batch and all positions, one pair per channel

questions.explanation: It mirrors weight sharing: one kernel applies everywhere, so its outputs are pooled everywhere.

questions.question: Batch normalisation starts with scale 1 and shift 0. Why those values?

options.B: They force zero mean output
options.C: They make it non-trainable
options.D: The layer starts as an identity on the normalised signal

questions.explanation: Starting from the identity means the layer does not disturb training before it has learned anything.

questions.question: Why is batch normalisation said to regularise a little?

options.A: Batch statistics inject noise into each sample
options.B: It lowers the learning rate
options.C: It adds a penalty to the loss
options.D: It removes parameters

questions.explanation: The effect fades as batches grow and the statistics settle.

questions.question: Why does data augmentation act as a regulariser?

options.A: It lowers the effective learning rate as training proceeds
options.B: It rescales the activations of each layer to zero mean
options.C: It reduces how many parameters the network has to fit
options.D: It widens the training distribution, so memorising gets harder

questions.explanation: It also builds in invariances you already know the task has.

questions.question: What is early stopping?

options.A: Cutting the learning rate when progress slows
options.B: Stopping when the training loss flattens
options.C: Stopping when validation stops improving, keeping the best model
options.D: Dropping layers during training

questions.explanation: It limits how far optimisation drifts from the starting point, which is a form of regularisation.

questions.question: What causes internal covariate shift?

options.B: Updates to earlier layers changing what later layers see
options.C: Test data drawn from another population
options.D: A learning rate that is too small

questions.explanation: The second option is dataset shift, a different problem entirely.

questions.question: What does the regularisation weight control?

options.A: How much the penalty counts against the data term
options.B: The batch size
options.C: The learning rate
options.D: The dropout fraction

questions.explanation: Too large and the model underfits, too small and it does nothing.

questions.question: Why do recurrent networks suit time series?

options.B: They need fewer parameters
options.C: They process all steps at once
options.D: Their state carries information between steps

questions.explanation: Ignoring order would defeat the point. The recurrence is exactly what makes them order-dependent.

questions.question: Image captioning takes one image and returns a sentence. What mapping is that?

options.B: Many to many
options.C: Many to one
options.D: One to many

questions.explanation: Sentiment scoring of a whole review is the mirror image, many to one.

questions.question: Your recurrent cell uses only ReLU and all weights and inputs are positive. What happens over a long sequence?

options.B: The state settles at 1
options.C: The state decays to zero
options.D: The state and gradients grow without limit

questions.explanation: A bounded activation like tanh prevents this, at the cost of making vanishing gradients the bigger risk.

questions.question: Why must weights not be updated step by step while backpropagating through time?

options.B: The learning rate would need rescaling
options.C: The function would change while it is being differentiated
options.D: The hidden state would reset

questions.explanation: Gradients are accumulated across all steps and applied once at the end.

questions.question: What does gradient clipping fix in a recurrent network?

options.B: Vanishing gradients
options.C: Class imbalance
options.D: Exploding gradients

questions.explanation: Vanishing gradients need architectural fixes such as gating, not clipping.

questions.question: What is the main thing an LSTM buys you over a simple recurrent cell?

options.A: No need for backpropagation through time
options.B: A gated cell state that carries gradients further
options.C: Fewer parameters
options.D: Faster inference

questions.explanation: The cell state is updated additively, so gradients are not squashed at every step.

questions.question: How does a GRU differ from an LSTM?

options.B: It merges gates and drops the separate cell state
options.C: It needs no training
options.D: It cannot model long dependencies

questions.explanation: Fewer parameters and slightly faster training, with accuracy usually close enough that the choice is empirical.

questions.question: What does a bidirectional recurrent network add?

options.B: Half the parameters
options.C: A reverse pass, so each step sees future context
options.D: Immunity to vanishing gradients

questions.explanation: It only works when the whole sequence is available up front, so it rules out live generation.

questions.question: What is the encoder's job in a sequence-to-sequence model?

options.B: Shuffling the data
options.C: Turning the input into a representation for the decoder
options.D: Generating the output tokens

questions.explanation: The split is what lets input and output have different lengths.

questions.question: Why was attention added to encoder-decoder models?

options.B: One fixed context vector is a bottleneck for long inputs
options.C: To cut the parameter count
options.D: To remove the encoder

questions.explanation: Parallel training came later with transformers. Attention was first bolted onto recurrent models.

questions.question: What is teacher forcing?

options.A: Raising the learning rate on the last layer of the decoder
options.B: Averaging the predictions of two separately trained models
options.C: Feeding the true previous token instead of the model's own guess
options.D: Freezing the encoder so only the decoder keeps training

questions.explanation: It speeds up early training but leaves a mismatch with inference, where only the model's own output exists.

questions.question: A recurrent output goes straight into cross entropy with no final activation. What is wrong?

options.B: Cross entropy is not differentiable
options.C: It expects probabilities, not unbounded values
options.D: Recurrent cells cannot classify

questions.explanation: The log in the loss is undefined for non-positive inputs.

questions.question: What does an autoencoder learn?

options.B: A map from inputs to labels
options.C: A distribution over labels
options.D: A compact code the input can be rebuilt from

questions.explanation: The bottleneck forces it to keep only what reconstruction needs.

questions.question: You build an autoencoder whose code is larger than its input, with no other constraint. What happens?

options.B: It can just copy the input and learn nothing
options.C: It becomes equivalent to PCA
options.D: It learns a richer representation

questions.explanation: An overcomplete autoencoder needs sparsity, noise or a penalty to force it to find structure.

questions.question: What makes a denoising autoencoder different?

options.B: It rebuilds clean input from a corrupted copy
options.C: It has no decoder
options.D: It uses labels

questions.explanation: Copying no longer works, so the model has to learn the structure of the data.

questions.question: What does a variational autoencoder encode each input to?

options.A: A single point in latent space
options.B: A binary code
options.C: A class label
options.D: A distribution over latent space

questions.explanation: Its loss pairs reconstruction with a term pulling those distributions towards a prior, which makes sampling possible.

questions.question: Why does a VAE need the reparameterisation trick?

options.B: To normalise the latent space
options.C: To make the decoder deterministic
options.D: Sampling blocks gradients, so the randomness is moved aside

questions.explanation: Writing the sample as mean plus sigma times noise leaves a differentiable path back to the encoder.

questions.question: What are the two networks in a GAN?

options.B: Generator and discriminator
options.C: Encoder and decoder
options.D: Teacher and student

questions.explanation: The generator makes samples from noise and the discriminator tries to spot them.

questions.question: What is mode collapse?

options.B: The weights go to zero
options.C: The generator only produces a narrow set of outputs
options.D: The loss goes negative

questions.explanation: A few samples reliably fool the discriminator, so the generator never explores further.

questions.question: How does a conditional GAN let you ask for a specific kind of sample?

options.B: By retraining for each request
options.C: By enlarging the noise vector
options.D: By feeding both networks a class input

questions.explanation: The training data has to be labelled so the discriminator can judge realism given the class.

questions.question: What is PCA for?

options.A: Finding directions of greatest variance to reduce dimensions
options.B: Generating new samples that resemble the training data
options.C: Penalising large weights so a network generalises better
options.D: Grouping data points into a fixed number of labelled classes

questions.explanation: The components are the leading eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.

questions.question: What does k-means do on each iteration?

options.A: Assigns points to the nearest centre, then moves the centres
options.B: Computes the eigenvectors of the data covariance matrix
options.C: Trains a small network to predict the cluster of each point
options.D: Assigns the true class label to every point in the set

questions.explanation: It reaches a local optimum that depends on where the centres started.

questions.question: What can DBSCAN do that k-means cannot?

options.B: Find non-spherical clusters without being told how many
options.C: Run faster on every dataset
options.D: Work without a distance measure

questions.explanation: The trade is that you now have to pick a density threshold and a radius instead.

questions.question: Why are learned word embeddings better than one-hot vectors?

options.B: They use less memory only
options.C: They need no data
options.D: Similar words end up close together

questions.explanation: One-hot vectors sit at equal distance from each other, so they carry no similarity information.

questions.question: In an encoder-decoder diagram, what does the narrow middle represent?

options.B: The loss function
options.C: The batch dimension
options.D: The bottleneck, or latent code

questions.explanation: Its size decides how much the model is allowed to keep.

questions.question: Why report the final score on a test set rather than the validation set?

options.B: Test sets are easier
options.C: Validation sets have no labels
options.D: Tuning on validation makes its score optimistic

questions.explanation: Every decision made by looking at a split leaks a little information about it.

questions.question: What does k-fold cross-validation give you?

options.B: More training data
options.C: A steadier estimate by averaging over splits
options.D: Immunity to overfitting

questions.explanation: Most useful on small datasets, where one split can be unlucky. The cost is training k times.

questions.question: When would you use over- or undersampling?

options.B: To generate synthetic data
options.C: To handle class imbalance
options.D: To tune dropout

questions.explanation: Weighting the loss by inverse class frequency does much the same without touching the data.

questions.question: Which metric suits a heavily imbalanced binary problem?

options.B: Training loss
options.C: F1 or area under the ROC curve
options.D: Parameter count

questions.explanation: Predicting the majority class for everything can score above 95% accuracy while detecting nothing.

questions.question: Why is random search often better than grid search over several hyperparameters?

options.A: Grid search wastes trials repeating values of the parameter that matters
options.B: It always reaches a good setting in fewer total trials
options.C: It is guaranteed to land on the global optimum eventually
options.D: It can be run without holding out any validation data

questions.explanation: Random search samples a fresh value on every axis, so it explores the important one far better.

questions.question: Your network was trained on 32x32 images and ends in flatten plus dense layers. How do you let it take any size?

options.A: Swap the dense layers for convolutions and global average pooling
options.B: Resize every incoming image down to 32x32 before the network
options.C: Train a separate copy of the network at each resolution
options.D: Add a recurrent layer that reads the feature map row by row

questions.explanation: Convolutions have no fixed input size and global pooling collapses whatever is left.

questions.question: Why normalise input features?

options.A: It removes outliers that would otherwise dominate the loss
options.B: It increases the range of functions the model can fit
options.C: Mismatched scales make the loss surface awkward to descend
options.D: It reduces the number of parameters the model has to learn

questions.explanation: A stretched loss surface makes gradient descent zig-zag and forces a smaller learning rate.

questions.question: Which statistics should normalise the test set?

options.B: Those of each sample separately
options.C: Those computed on the test set
options.D: Those computed on the training set

questions.explanation: Using test statistics would apply a different transform than the model was trained under.

questions.question: Why switch a model to evaluation mode before inference?

options.B: To free memory
options.C: So dropout and batch norm behave correctly
options.D: To reset the weights

questions.explanation: Forget it and dropout stays active, making predictions noisy and irreproducible.

questions.question: Your training loss falls while validation loss climbs from epoch 12. What do you do?

options.A: Stop around epoch 12 and keep that model
options.B: Raise the learning rate
options.C: Train longer
options.D: Enlarge the validation set

questions.explanation: The point where the two curves part is exactly what early stopping looks for.

questions.question: The loss does not move at all from the first iteration. What is the likely cause?

options.B: The updates are not reaching the parameters
options.C: The batch size is too large
options.D: The validation set is small

questions.explanation: A perfectly flat loss usually means a zero learning rate or an optimizer that never got the parameters.

questions.question: What was ResNet's key idea?

options.B: Parallel paths with different kernel sizes
options.C: Dropping pooling entirely
options.D: Learning a residual on top of the identity

questions.explanation: That is what made networks over a hundred layers deep trainable.

questions.question: What is VGG known for?

options.B: Introducing attention
options.C: Introducing skip connections
options.D: Stacking small 3x3 convolutions instead of large kernels

questions.explanation: Its uniform design made it a popular backbone, though its dense layers are parameter heavy.

questions.question: What does an Inception module do?

options.B: Adds recurrence
options.C: Removes all pooling
options.D: Runs several kernel sizes side by side and concatenates

questions.explanation: 1x1 convolutions cut the depth first so the wider kernels stay affordable.

questions.question: Inside a residual block the stride and channel count change. What happens to the shortcut?

options.B: The block splits in two
options.C: It gets a 1x1 convolution to match shapes
options.D: It becomes a concatenation

questions.explanation: Addition needs matching shapes on both branches.

questions.question: What is the core mechanism of a transformer?

options.A: Self-attention over all positions at once
options.B: Clustering of tokens
options.C: Recurrence
options.D: Dilated convolution

questions.explanation: Constant path length between positions avoids the long-range decay that limits recurrent models.

questions.question: How does a Vision Transformer feed an image to the model?

options.B: The whole image as one token
options.C: One token per fixed-size patch
options.D: One token per pixel

questions.explanation: Attention cost grows quadratically with length, so a token per pixel would be far too expensive.

questions.question: What does global average pooling produce from C feature maps?

options.B: A single number
options.C: A map at half the size
options.D: One value per pixel

questions.explanation: Because the spatial extent disappears, the classifier stops depending on input resolution.

questions.question: Two stacked 3x3 convolutions replace one 5x5. What is gained?

options.A: Fewer parameters and an extra non-linearity
options.B: Fewer channels
options.C: Faster inference only
options.D: A wider receptive field

questions.explanation: Both cover 5x5, but the pair uses 18 weights per channel pair instead of 25.

questions.question: What does a saliency map show?

options.B: The loss over time
options.C: The label distribution
options.D: Which pixels the prediction is most sensitive to

questions.explanation: A quick way to check the model is looking at the object and not the background.

questions.question: What is Grad-CAM for?

options.B: Compressing a model
options.C: A class-specific heat map from a convolutional layer
options.D: Speeding up training

questions.explanation: It weights feature maps by their gradients, so the result is coarse but tied to one class.

questions.question: Deeper kernels are hard to read directly. What do people look at instead?

options.A: The activations for a given input
options.B: The batch statistics
options.C: The bias values
options.D: The learning rate

questions.explanation: Activations show what the layer responds to on a real image.

questions.question: In self-attention, where do query, key and value come from?

options.A: Three learned projections of the same input
options.B: A fixed lookup table
options.C: Three separately trained networks
options.D: The labels

questions.explanation: Attending to projections of the same sequence is what makes it self-attention.

questions.question: Why do transformers need positional encoding?

options.B: To cut parameters
options.C: Attention alone would treat a shuffled sequence the same
options.D: To normalise attention weights

questions.explanation: Recurrent and convolutional models get position from their structure. Attention does not.

questions.question: What does multi-head attention add over one head?

options.B: Several attention patterns in different subspaces
options.C: Guaranteed sparse weights
options.D: Freedom from positional encoding

questions.explanation: One head might track nearby words, another long-range links, which a single averaged head would blur.

questions.question: In an attention diagram, what does a thicker line between two tokens mean?

options.B: A gradient path
options.C: A larger attention weight
options.D: A longer distance

questions.explanation: Line thickness or opacity is the usual way to draw how much one token attends to another.

questions.question: What does Q-learning estimate?

options.B: The transition probabilities
options.C: The reward function
options.D: The expected return of an action in a state

questions.explanation: The policy follows by taking the argmax, so no model of the environment is needed.

questions.question: What is the difference between exploration and exploitation?

options.A: Exploitation takes the best known action, exploration tries others
options.B: Exploration maximises immediate reward
options.C: Exploitation is for training, exploration for testing
options.D: They mean the same thing

questions.explanation: A purely exploitative agent can settle on a decent action and never find a better one.

questions.question: What does epsilon-greedy do?

options.B: Picks randomly with probability epsilon, greedily otherwise
options.C: Explores only in episode one
options.D: Always picks the best-valued action

questions.explanation: Epsilon is usually annealed so the agent explores early and exploits later.

questions.question: Why does DQN keep a replay buffer?

options.A: Consecutive transitions are correlated, and sampling breaks that up
options.B: To cut the memory the agent needs while it is learning
options.C: To remove the need for a separate target network
options.D: To make the reward signal differentiable end to end

questions.explanation: It also lets each transition be reused, which matters when interaction is expensive.

questions.question: What is the target network in DQN for?

options.B: Holding the bootstrap target still between updates
options.C: Choosing actions
options.D: Computing rewards

questions.explanation: Bootstrapping from a network that changes every step means chasing a moving target.

questions.question: What does the discount factor do?

options.A: Weights near rewards above distant ones and keeps the return finite
options.B: Sets how large a step each value update takes
options.C: Rescales every reward into the range 0 to 1 before use
options.D: Decides how often the agent picks a random action

questions.explanation: Close to 1 makes the agent far-sighted, small values make it short-sighted.

questions.question: Which claim about reinforcement learning is wrong?

options.A: Bellman equations are solvable exactly for small problems
options.B: Q-learning is off-policy
options.C: Temporal difference learning needs the environment dynamics
options.D: A good policy maximises expected future return

questions.explanation: Temporal difference learning is model-free and bootstraps from sampled transitions.

questions.question: What do actor-critic methods combine?

options.B: Two value functions
options.C: A policy that acts with a value function that judges
options.D: Supervised and unsupervised learning

questions.explanation: The critic gives the actor a lower-variance signal than the raw return would.

questions.question: What does semantic segmentation produce?

options.B: A class label for every pixel
options.C: A box around each object
options.D: A separate mask per object

questions.explanation: It does not tell two objects of the same class apart. That is instance segmentation.

questions.question: How does instance segmentation differ from semantic segmentation?

options.B: It uses no convolutions
options.C: It only outputs boxes
options.D: It separates individual objects of the same class

questions.explanation: Two touching cars become one region under semantic segmentation but two masks under instance.

questions.question: You need to count people in a room. Which task fits?

options.B: Object detection
options.C: Image classification
options.D: Semantic segmentation

questions.explanation: Segmentation merges everyone into one region, so overlapping people cannot be counted.

questions.question: What defines U-Net?

options.A: Skip connections from the encoder to the decoder
options.B: Only dense layers
options.C: It has no downsampling
options.D: No training needed

questions.explanation: Without them the decoder knows roughly where an object is but not where its edge is.

questions.question: What does a fully convolutional network replace to give a dense output?

options.B: The loss
options.C: The pooling layers
options.D: The activations

questions.explanation: Dense layers fix the input size and throw away spatial layout.

questions.question: Can a segmentation mask be turned into a bounding box?

options.B: Yes, by taking the extent of the region
options.C: Only if it is binary
options.D: Only for convex shapes

questions.explanation: It works one way only, which is why masks are the more expensive annotation.

questions.question: What does panoptic segmentation combine?

options.A: Semantic labels for regions with instance masks for objects
options.B: The training and inference passes into a single network
options.C: Two separate architectures whose outputs are averaged
options.D: A classification head with a regression head for box corners

questions.explanation: Sky and road are handled semantically, cars and people get their own masks.

questions.question: What is a training sample made of in supervised learning?

options.A: A batch of inputs sharing a target
options.B: An input with no target attached
options.C: An input paired with its target
options.D: A target with no matching input

questions.explanation: Without the target there is nothing to compute a loss against.

questions.question: What is an activation function for?

options.B: Scaling the learning rate
options.C: Adding non-linearity between layers
options.D: Normalising the input

questions.explanation: Without one, stacking layers gains you nothing.

questions.question: What is a hyperparameter?

options.A: A weight the optimizer adjusts during training
options.B: The value the loss function returns each step
options.C: A setting chosen before training, like the learning rate
options.D: A gradient computed during the backward pass

questions.explanation: Weights are learned, hyperparameters are picked by you or by a search.

questions.question: Your training and validation losses are both high and close together. What is going on?

options.A: The learning rate is too small only
options.B: Data leakage
options.C: Overfitting
options.D: Underfitting

questions.explanation: Overfitting shows as a gap between the two. Both high means the model is not fitting at all yet.

questions.question: What is a mini-batch?

options.A: A small subset of the training set used for one update
options.B: A shortened training run used to check the setup
options.C: A network with fewer layers than the full model
options.D: A reduced set of classes used for a quick test

questions.explanation: It trades gradient accuracy for many more updates per epoch.

questions.question: You get near-perfect validation accuracy on the first epoch. What should you check?

options.A: Whether training data leaked into validation
options.B: Whether the learning rate is too high
options.C: Whether the batch size is too small
options.D: Whether dropout is on

questions.explanation: Results that look too good usually mean the split is broken, not that the model is brilliant.

questions.question: What is the bias-variance trade-off about?

options.B: Training against inference
options.C: Systematic error against sensitivity to the training set
options.D: Speed against memory

questions.explanation: Simple models err systematically, flexible ones swing with whichever data they saw.

questions.question: What does a layer's weight matrix do to its input?

options.B: Removes negatives
options.C: Sorts the values
options.D: Applies a linear map

questions.explanation: The bias then shifts it, and the activation adds the non-linearity.

questions.question: Why are weights initialised randomly?

options.B: To cut the parameter count
options.C: To speed up the forward pass
options.D: To break symmetry between units

questions.explanation: Identical starting weights leave every unit in a layer learning the same thing.

questions.question: What is Xavier initialisation trying to keep steady?

options.B: The loss value
options.C: The variance of the signal through the layers
options.D: The number of active units

questions.explanation: Shrink it and the signal dies out, grow it and the activations explode.

questions.question: Why does He initialisation use a factor of 2 where Xavier does not?

options.B: It assumes mini-batches
options.C: It assumes a bias term
options.D: It assumes ReLU, which discards half the signal

questions.explanation: Doubling the initial variance makes up for what the ReLU cuts away.

questions.question: What does an optimizer need besides the current weights?

options.A: The score the model reached on validation
options.B: The full training set it is fitting to
options.C: The layer structure of the whole network
options.D: The gradient of the loss for those weights

questions.explanation: That separation is what lets one optimizer serve any layer type.

questions.question: What is a gradient?

options.A: The direction of steepest increase of the loss
options.B: The current numerical value of the weight
options.C: The value the loss function currently takes
options.D: The step size used when updating the weights

questions.explanation: Which is why descent moves in the opposite direction.

questions.question: What is the output range of a sigmoid?

options.B: 0 to infinity
options.C: -infinity to infinity
options.D: -1 to 1

questions.explanation: Which is why it can stand in for a probability. Tanh covers -1 to 1.

questions.question: What is the output range of tanh?

options.B: -1 to 1
options.C: 0 to infinity
options.D: -infinity to infinity

questions.explanation: Being zero-centred is one reason it is often preferred to sigmoid inside a network.

questions.question: What does same padding with stride 1 achieve?

options.B: The output keeps the input size
options.C: The channels double
options.D: The output shrinks

questions.explanation: Zero padding replaces the border pixels the kernel would otherwise cost you.

questions.question: What does dropout do during training?

options.B: Deletes units permanently
options.C: Switches off a random subset each iteration
options.D: Normalises activations

questions.explanation: A different subset each time, so the network behaves like an ensemble of thinner ones.

questions.question: What problem do long sequences cause when training recurrent networks?

options.B: Gradients vanish or explode
options.C: The loss becomes non-differentiable
options.D: The hidden state disappears

questions.explanation: Repeated multiplication through time either shrinks or amplifies the signal.

questions.question: What is truncated backpropagation through time?

options.B: Dropping the hidden state
options.C: Limiting how many steps back the gradient is traced
options.D: Removing the recurrence

questions.explanation: It bounds the memory and compute a long sequence would otherwise need.

questions.question: What kind of data does unsupervised learning work with?

options.B: Unlabelled data
options.C: Image data only
options.D: Labelled data only

questions.explanation: The objective comes from the data itself rather than from targets.

questions.question: What does the generator in a GAN take as input?

options.B: Class labels only
options.C: The discriminator's weights
options.D: A random noise vector

questions.explanation: It learns to map that noise onto something that passes for real data.

questions.question: Why is GAN training often unstable?

options.B: The two networks chase each other rather than a fixed target
options.C: The loss is not differentiable
options.D: The data is unlabelled

questions.explanation: Each network's objective shifts as the other improves, so there is no fixed thing to converge to.

questions.question: How would you pick k for k-means when it is not known?

options.B: Try several and look for a knee in the error curve
options.C: Use the number of features
options.D: Always use k equal to 2

questions.explanation: The elbow method and silhouette scores are the usual heuristics.

questions.question: A linear autoencoder trained with squared error learns a subspace. Which one?

options.B: The null space of the data
options.C: A random subspace
options.D: The full input space

questions.explanation: Without a non-linearity it can do no better than the leading principal components.

questions.question: What does the latent space of a generative model let you do?

options.B: Label the training data
options.C: Remove the decoder
options.D: Sample new data by drawing a code and decoding it

questions.explanation: Which is why a VAE bothers to shape that space towards a known prior.

questions.question: What does occlusion testing involve?

options.A: Hiding the labels and checking the model still agrees
options.B: Removing a layer and measuring how much accuracy drops
options.C: Dropping training samples to see which ones mattered
options.D: Blocking part of the image and watching the prediction change

questions.explanation: Wherever covering the image hurts the score most is where the model was looking.

questions.question: What do first-layer convolution kernels typically learn on natural images?

options.A: Whole objects such as faces or cars
options.B: The class labels used in training
options.C: Edges and simple colour blobs
options.D: Patterns with no visible structure

questions.explanation: Later layers combine these into textures, parts and eventually objects.

questions.question: Why do people visualise networks at all?

options.B: To reduce parameters
options.C: To speed up inference
options.D: To check the model is using sensible evidence

questions.explanation: A model can be right for the wrong reason, and visualisation is how you catch that.

questions.question: Attention weights across a sequence must satisfy which property?

options.A: They sum to one over the attended positions
options.B: They are binary
options.C: They are negative
options.D: They are all equal

questions.explanation: They come from a softmax, so they form a distribution over positions.

questions.question: What does an agent receive from the environment after acting?

options.A: A new state and a reward
options.B: A label for the correct action
options.C: A batch of training examples
options.D: A gradient for its parameters

questions.explanation: That loop of state, action, reward is the whole setting.

questions.question: What is a policy?

options.A: The function giving reward for each step
options.B: The factor discounting distant rewards
options.C: The model of how the environment moves
options.D: A rule mapping states to actions

questions.explanation: Learning a good one is the point of the whole exercise.

questions.question: What makes Q-learning off-policy?

options.B: It learns about the greedy policy while acting differently
options.C: It cannot use a neural network
options.D: It works only offline

questions.explanation: Which is what lets it learn from a replay buffer of older behaviour.

questions.question: Why is reinforcement learning harder than supervised learning?

options.A: The objective cannot be optimised with gradient methods
options.B: The observations are always images, which are costly to process
options.C: The networks involved are much larger than in supervised work
options.D: The reward may be delayed and there is no labelled correct action

questions.explanation: Credit assignment across a long sequence of actions is the core difficulty.

questions.question: What does depth mean in a network?

options.B: The batch size
options.C: The number of classes
options.D: The number of layers between input and output

questions.explanation: Width is the number of units or channels in a layer.

questions.question: Why do modern architectures often drop large dense layers at the end?

options.A: They produce no usable gradient for the layers beneath them
options.B: They hold most of the parameters and tie the model to one input size
options.C: They are far slower to compute than an equivalent convolution
options.D: They cannot be trained by backpropagation like other layers

questions.explanation: Global average pooling replaces them and removes both problems at once.

questions.question: What is a backbone in a vision model?

options.B: The feature extractor shared across tasks
options.C: The loss function
options.D: The optimizer

questions.explanation: Detection and segmentation models bolt task-specific heads onto a common backbone.

questions.question: What does object detection output?

options.A: A reconstruction of the original image
options.B: A class label for every pixel in the image
options.C: A box and a class for each object found
options.D: A single class label for the whole image

questions.explanation: Because it gives one entry per object, its output can be counted.

questions.question: What does IoU measure?

options.A: How long the model took to finish training
options.B: How many distinct classes the model can output
options.C: The value the loss function reached at the end
options.D: Overlap between the predicted and true regions

questions.explanation: Intersection divided by union, so 1 means a perfect match.

questions.question: Why is the encoder-decoder shape common in segmentation?

options.A: The encoder builds context, the decoder restores resolution
options.B: It roughly halves the parameters a flat network would need
options.C: It trains considerably faster than a flat convolutional stack
options.D: It removes the need for pixel-level labels during training

questions.explanation: Downsampling widens the receptive field, and the decoder puts the detail back.

questions.question: What does a confusion matrix show?

options.A: How the loss changed over training time
options.B: Predicted classes against the true ones
options.C: The distribution of the learned weights
options.D: The size of the gradients in each layer

questions.explanation: It exposes which classes get mistaken for which, unlike a single accuracy number.

questions.question: What is precision?

options.A: The area beneath the receiver operating curve
options.B: Correct positives out of all actual positives
options.C: All correct predictions over the whole test set
options.D: Correct positives out of all predicted positives

questions.explanation: Recall is the other one, correct positives out of all actual positives.

questions.question: What is transfer learning?

options.A: Moving a trained model to another machine
options.B: Sharing training data between two research teams
options.C: Converting a model into a different file format
options.D: Reusing weights from one task on another

questions.explanation: Most useful when the new task has too little data to learn features from scratch.

questions.question: When fine-tuning a pretrained network on a small dataset, what is common practice?

options.B: Train only the input layer
options.C: Freeze the early layers and train the later ones
options.D: Retrain everything at a high learning rate

questions.explanation: Early features are generic and a small dataset would only damage them.

questions.question: Which statement about weight initialisation is FALSE?

options.A: Initialisation matters because the loss surface is convex
options.B: All-zero weights leave every unit with the same gradient
options.C: A small positive bias can help against dying ReLUs
options.D: Xavier takes the layer's input size into account

questions.explanation: Deep network losses are not convex. That is part of why the starting point matters.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a reason convolutions suit images?

options.A: The same pattern can appear anywhere
options.B: Images always have exactly three channels
options.C: Nearby pixels are related
options.D: Weight sharing keeps the parameter count down

questions.explanation: Grayscale, depth maps and multispectral images all work fine with convolutions.

questions.question: Which statement about loss functions is FALSE?

options.A: Cross entropy pairs naturally with a softmax output
options.B: The loss must return one number per mini-batch
options.C: Squared error suits regression with Gaussian noise
options.D: Any loss works with any output activation

questions.explanation: Cross entropy needs values in a valid probability range, which is why the output activation is not a free choice.

questions.question: Which statement about dropout is FALSE?

options.A: It drops a different random subset each iteration
options.B: It discourages units from co-adapting
options.C: It is switched off during inference
options.D: It fixes vanishing gradients

questions.explanation: Dropout targets overfitting. Vanishing gradients need better activations or skip connections.

questions.question: Which statement about recurrent networks is FALSE?

options.A: The hidden state carries information forward
options.B: Long sequences worsen gradient problems
options.C: The same weights are reused at every time step
options.D: Every time step is processed in parallel

questions.explanation: Each step needs the previous hidden state, so the recurrence is inherently sequential.

questions.question: Which statement about autoencoders is FALSE?

options.A: They are trained without class labels
options.B: They always need a larger code than input
options.C: The bottleneck limits what can be kept
options.D: The decoder rebuilds the input from the code

questions.explanation: An overcomplete code is possible but needs an extra constraint, otherwise the model just copies its input.

questions.question: Which statement about segmentation is FALSE?

options.A: A mask carries less information than a bounding box
options.B: IoU is a sensible evaluation measure
options.C: Semantic segmentation labels every pixel
options.D: Instance segmentation separates objects of one class

questions.explanation: It is the other way round. A mask can be reduced to a box, but not the reverse.

questions.question: Which statement about reinforcement learning is FALSE?

options.A: Rewards can be delayed by many steps
options.B: Exploration matters most during training
options.C: The agent learns from rewards, not labelled actions
options.D: The optimal policy is known in advance

questions.explanation: If it were known there would be nothing left to learn.

questions.question: Which statement about ResNet is FALSE?

options.A: A block can fall back to the identity
options.B: Skip connections let gradients bypass a block
options.C: It made very deep networks trainable
options.D: It removed the need for any normalisation

questions.explanation: ResNet blocks use batch normalisation throughout.

questions.question: You double the size of your training set and validation accuracy improves noticeably. What does that suggest?

options.A: The model was mainly limited by data, not capacity
options.B: The validation split is broken
options.C: The learning rate was too high
options.D: The model is underfitting badly

questions.explanation: If capacity were the binding constraint, extra data would have made much less difference.

questions.question: You train the same network twice with different random seeds and get noticeably different test scores. What is the most reasonable response?

options.A: Fix the seed and never mention it
options.B: Conclude the network is broken
options.C: Report the average and spread over several runs
options.D: Report whichever run scored highest

questions.explanation: A single run tells you as much about the seed as about the model.

questions.question: Your loss drops fast for a few hundred steps then starts climbing steadily. What would you try first?

options.B: Remove the validation set
options.C: Increase the learning rate
options.D: Add more layers to the network

questions.explanation: A loss that falls then rises usually means the steps are too big to settle once it nears a minimum.

questions.question: You add dropout and both training and validation accuracy get worse. What is the likely explanation?

options.B: The model was not overfitting, so the extra noise only hurts
options.C: Dropout was applied at inference by mistake
options.D: The learning rate must be raised to compensate

questions.explanation: Regularisation costs capacity, which is only worth paying when there is overfitting to fix.

questions.question: You replace a 5x5 convolution with two stacked 3x3 convolutions and accuracy holds while parameters drop. Why does that work?

options.A: The two layers cover the same receptive field
options.B: 3x3 kernels need no padding
options.C: Smaller kernels always generalise better
options.D: The stride compensates for the change

questions.explanation: Two 3x3 layers see 5x5 between them, with fewer weights and an extra non-linearity thrown in.

questions.question: Your model does well on validation but poorly once deployed. Which explanation is most plausible?

options.A: The learning rate was too low
options.B: Live data differs from the data the splits came from
options.C: The batch size was too small
options.D: The model has too few parameters

questions.explanation: This is dataset shift. Validation only tells you about the distribution it was drawn from.

questions.question: Your recurrent model handles short sequences well but fails on long ones. What is the most likely cause?

options.B: The hidden state is too small to store the input
options.C: Gradients vanish over the longer span
options.D: The batch size is too large

questions.explanation: Gating or attention are the usual answers when longer contexts stop working.

questions.question: Your GAN produces convincing images but they all look nearly the same. What has happened?

options.B: Mode collapse
options.C: Overfitting to the validation set
options.D: Vanishing gradients

questions.explanation: The generator found a few outputs that always fool the discriminator and stopped exploring.

questions.question: You get 97% accuracy on a dataset where 97% of samples are negative. What should you conclude?

options.A: The model may be predicting the majority class every time
options.B: The model has learned the task well
options.C: The dataset is too small
options.D: The learning rate is correct

questions.explanation: Check precision, recall or the confusion matrix before believing an accuracy figure on imbalanced data.

questions.question: Your segmentation model finds objects in roughly the right place but the edges are badly blurred. What usually helps?

options.B: A smaller learning rate
options.C: Skip connections carrying fine detail to the decoder
options.D: Removing the encoder

questions.explanation: Downsampling throws away the detail that sharp boundaries need, and skip connections put it back.

questions.question: You make a plain network deeper and the training error rises. What does that tell you?

options.B: The deeper network is overfitting
options.C: The learning rate is too low
options.D: The deeper network is harder to optimise

questions.explanation: Overfitting would lower the training error. This is the degradation problem residual connections were built for.

questions.question: A saliency map shows your classifier attending to the background rather than the animal. What does that suggest?

options.B: The learning rate is too high
options.C: The model has latched onto a confound
options.D: The model needs more layers

questions.explanation: Its accuracy is real but will not survive backgrounds that break the correlation.

questions.question: You remove all activation functions from a ten-layer network and accuracy collapses to that of a linear model. Why?

options.B: The loss stops being differentiable
options.C: The whole stack collapses into a single linear map
options.D: The weights become zero

questions.explanation: Without a non-linearity anywhere, ten layers express exactly what one can.

questions.question: You switch from batch size 256 to batch size 8 and keep everything else fixed. What should you expect?

options.B: A smoother loss curve
options.C: Fewer updates per epoch
options.D: A noisier loss curve and more updates per epoch

questions.explanation: Smaller batches give a noisier gradient estimate, and the learning rate often needs adjusting too.

questions.question: You apply batch normalisation and find you can raise the learning rate considerably. Why?

options.B: Layer inputs stay on a consistent scale
options.C: The parameter count drops
options.D: The gradients become larger

questions.explanation: A big step in one layer no longer throws off everything above it.

questions.question: Which of these does NOT help against overfitting?

options.A: Adding weight decay to the optimizer
options.B: Training for many more epochs
options.C: Adding dropout to the dense layers
options.D: Augmenting the training images

questions.explanation: Training longer moves you further into overfitting once validation loss turns.

questions.question: Which statement about a convolutional layer is FALSE?

options.A: Its weight count depends on the stride
options.B: Its weight count depends on the kernel size
options.C: Its weight count depends on the input channels
options.D: It can handle inputs of different spatial sizes

questions.explanation: Stride changes where the kernel is evaluated, not how many weights it holds.
