questions.question: Which property do convolutional networks NOT rely on?

options.B: Every pixel connecting to every other
options.C: The same kernel being useful anywhere
options.D: Nearby pixels being related

questions.explanation: Full connectivity is exactly what convolution replaces, and why it needs so few weights.

questions.question: What does the weight count of a convolutional layer depend on?

options.B: The batch size
options.C: The size of the input image
options.D: The number of kernels

questions.explanation: It depends on kernel size, input channels and kernel count. Nothing else.

questions.question: Which statement about pooling is FALSE?

options.B: It has no trainable parameters
options.C: It shrinks the spatial dimensions
options.D: It gives some tolerance to small shifts

questions.explanation: Pooling works inside each channel, so the depth comes out unchanged.

questions.question: Which statement about ReLU is FALSE?

options.A: Its output is squashed between 0 and 1
options.B: Its gradient is 1 for positive inputs
options.C: Units stuck negative stop receiving gradient
options.D: It has no parameters to learn

questions.explanation: That range describes the sigmoid. ReLU has no upper bound at all.

questions.question: Which statement about activation functions is FALSE?

options.A: Softmax is normally used on hidden layers
options.B: ReLU can leave units permanently inactive
options.C: Tanh is zero-centred and sigmoid is not
options.D: Sigmoid outputs lie between 0 and 1

questions.explanation: Softmax belongs at the output of a classifier, where you want a distribution over classes.

questions.question: Which statement about batch normalisation is FALSE?

options.A: It has a learned scale and shift
options.B: It normalises what the next layer receives
options.C: It normalises the weights of each layer
options.D: It uses running statistics at inference

questions.explanation: It works on activations. The weights are left to the optimizer and any weight decay.

questions.question: Which statement about batch normalisation is TRUE?

options.A: It rescales the weights rather than the activations
options.B: It smooths the loss surface, so larger steps work
options.C: It can be left out entirely at inference
options.D: It removes the need for an activation function

questions.explanation: Skipping it at inference would break the network, since it was trained with the transform in place.

questions.question: Which of these does NOT contribute to internal covariate shift?

options.A: Fixing the random seed before training
options.B: Large steps causing big parameter changes
options.C: Input features left on very different scales
options.D: Earlier layers updating and shifting later inputs

questions.explanation: The seed fixes initialisation and shuffling order. It has nothing to do with distributions drifting.

questions.question: Which layer behaves differently during training and inference?

options.B: Dropout
options.C: Max pooling
options.D: Fully connected

questions.explanation: Batch normalisation is the other one, switching from batch to running statistics.

questions.question: Which statement comparing L1 and L2 is FALSE?

options.A: Both add a term to the loss and gradient
options.B: L1 drives many weights to exactly zero
options.C: L2 shrinks the largest weights hardest
options.D: L1 is differentiable everywhere

questions.explanation: The absolute value has a kink at zero, so L1 needs a sub-gradient there.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a regularisation technique?

options.B: Raising the learning rate
options.C: Weight decay
options.D: Data augmentation

questions.explanation: A larger learning rate destabilises training rather than restricting the model.

questions.question: Which statement about adversarial examples is FALSE?

options.A: They affect other methods such as SVMs too
options.B: A tiny change can flip the prediction
options.C: They are produced by a GAN generator
options.D: The perturbation can be hard to see

questions.explanation: They come from optimising the input against the model, not from a GAN.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a problem with the simple Elman cell?

options.A: Losing information from early in the sequence
options.B: Struggling to train on long sequences
options.C: Gradients shrinking over many time steps
options.D: Being unable to map many inputs to many outputs

questions.explanation: Many-to-many is a standard use. The real trouble is what happens to gradients over a long span.

questions.question: Which statement about the LSTM is TRUE?

options.A: It processes all time steps at once
options.B: It has fewer parameters than a simple cell
options.C: It avoids backpropagation through time
options.D: Its cell state is updated additively

questions.explanation: That additive path is what lets gradients survive a long sequence. Gates cost extra parameters.

questions.question: Which statement about LSTMs is FALSE?

options.A: The cell state is updated by addition
options.B: They hold more parameters than a simple cell
options.C: They are trained without unrolling over time
options.D: Gates decide what enters and leaves the cell state

questions.explanation: They are still unrolled and trained with backpropagation through time.

questions.question: Which statement about backpropagation through time is FALSE?

options.A: Truncating it limits the memory needed
options.B: Gradients are summed over the time steps
options.C: Every time step has its own weight set
options.D: The network is unrolled across the sequence

questions.explanation: Sharing one weight set across time is what makes the network recurrent.

questions.question: Which architecture uses both convolutions and skip connections?

options.B: AlexNet
options.C: VGG16
options.D: ResNet

questions.explanation: U-Net is another. VGG, LeNet and AlexNet are all plain stacks.

questions.question: Which feature is NOT used in LeNet?

options.B: Saturating activations
options.C: Pooling layers
options.D: Skip connections

questions.explanation: LeNet predates ResNet. It also used tanh and sigmoid rather than ReLU.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT an advantage of making a network deeper?

options.A: It uses less memory than a shallow one
options.B: Earlier features get reused by later layers
options.C: More complex functions can be fitted
options.D: Features become more abstract with depth

questions.explanation: More layers means more activations to store, so memory goes up rather than down.

questions.question: Which statement about segmentation is TRUE?

options.A: Semantic segmentation is classification per pixel
options.B: Instance segmentation finds one object per class
options.C: The aim of segmentation is drawing boxes
options.D: A mask cannot be turned into a bounding box

questions.explanation: A mask converts to a box easily. It is the reverse that loses information.

questions.question: Which measure is NOT used to evaluate semantic segmentation?

options.B: Pixel accuracy
options.C: Frequency weighted IoU
options.D: Perplexity

questions.explanation: Perplexity belongs to language modelling, where it scores a predicted distribution over tokens.

questions.question: Which is NOT a reason to avoid classifying each pixel with dense layers?

options.A: The context around the pixel is thrown away
options.B: Dense layers cannot output many classes
options.C: Weight sharing across positions is lost
options.D: The parameter count becomes enormous

questions.explanation: The class count is not the obstacle. The lost context and the parameter blow-up are.

questions.question: Which statement about model capacity is FALSE?

options.A: It relates to the range of functions available
options.B: Raising it necessarily raises the bias
options.C: Depth is one thing that increases it
options.D: It sits at the heart of the bias-variance trade-off

questions.explanation: More capacity usually lowers bias and raises variance, so that has it backwards.

questions.question: Which statement about the forward and backward passes is FALSE?

options.A: Layers with parameters also get a weight gradient
options.B: The backward pass returns the input gradient
options.C: The forward pass turns input into output
options.D: The backward pass needs nothing from the forward pass

questions.explanation: A dense layer has to keep its input, and ReLU has to remember which entries were positive.

questions.question: Which statement about vanishing gradients is FALSE?

options.A: Dropout is the usual way to fix it
options.B: Saturating activations make it worse
options.C: Long sequences make it more likely
options.D: Early layers learn more slowly than late ones

questions.explanation: Dropout is aimed at overfitting. Better activations, normalisation and skip connections are the fix here.

questions.question: Which statement about momentum is FALSE?

options.A: It builds a velocity from earlier gradients
options.B: Values near 0.9 are commonly used
options.C: It removes the need for a learning rate
options.D: It damps oscillation across a ravine

questions.explanation: You still set a learning rate. Momentum changes how the gradient is applied, not the step scale.

questions.question: Which statement about SGD compared to full-batch descent is FALSE?

options.A: Its noise can help escape poor minima
options.B: Its gradient estimate is noisier
options.C: It makes many more updates per epoch
options.D: Full-batch descent needs the data shuffled

questions.explanation: Full-batch uses the whole set for every update, so the order it sees the data in is irrelevant.

questions.question: Which statement about the learning rate is FALSE?

options.A: Decaying it late helps the model settle
options.B: Adam removes the need to set one
options.C: Too small a rate makes training crawl
options.D: Too large a rate can make the loss diverge

questions.explanation: Adam adapts the step per parameter but still takes a base rate from you.

questions.question: Training loss sits high and flat from the first epoch. Which is NOT worth checking?

options.B: Whether the labels line up with the inputs
options.C: Whether the model has enough capacity
options.D: Whether the learning rate is far too small

questions.explanation: A high flat loss means underfitting or a bug. Dropout would only make it worse.

questions.question: Which statement about shuffling the training data is FALSE?

options.A: Sorted data gives biased batch gradients
options.B: It does not change the loss being minimised
options.C: It stops a sample appearing twice in an epoch
options.D: It should be redone at the start of each epoch

questions.explanation: An epoch visits each sample once either way. Shuffling changes the order, not the contents.

questions.question: Which statement about normalising input data is FALSE?

options.B: Test data uses the training statistics
options.C: Each sample should be normalised on its own
options.D: Zero mean and unit variance is a common choice

questions.explanation: Per-sample normalisation would erase real differences between samples and apply a different transform to each.

questions.question: Which should you do before running a validation pass?

options.A: Zero the weights of the last layer
options.B: Apply the training augmentations too
options.C: Switch the model into evaluation mode
options.D: Reset the optimizer's internal state

questions.explanation: Turning off gradients is the other one. Augmenting validation would make the score wobble run to run.

questions.question: Which statement about splitting your data is FALSE?

options.A: The test set should be used for early stopping
options.B: Normalisation uses training statistics only
options.C: The test set is touched once, at the end
options.D: Validation guides choices during development

questions.explanation: Stopping on the test set spends the one split meant to give you an honest final number.

questions.question: Which statement about transfer learning is FALSE?

options.A: Early layers learn features that transfer
options.B: The final layer is usually replaced
options.C: Both tasks must share the same labels
options.D: It helps most when the new dataset is small

questions.explanation: Identical labels would make the whole exercise pointless.

questions.question: Which of these does NOT separate deep learning from classical machine learning?

options.A: Features are learned rather than designed
options.B: Representation and classifier train together
options.C: No optimisation is involved
options.D: Considerably more data is usually needed

questions.explanation: Deep models are still fitted by optimisation. What changed is that nobody hand-designs the features.

questions.question: Which of these did NOT drive the deep learning breakthrough around 2012?

options.A: Large labelled datasets such as ImageNet
options.B: General purpose computation on GPUs
options.C: Activations that do not saturate
options.D: The first publication of backpropagation

questions.explanation: Backpropagation was decades old by then. Scale and a few training tricks were what changed.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT an unsupervised objective?

options.B: Cross entropy against human labels
options.C: Rebuilding the input through a bottleneck
options.D: Matching a generated distribution to the data

questions.explanation: That is the definition of supervised learning. The others take their signal from the data itself.

questions.question: Which statement about GANs is FALSE?

options.B: The generator starts from random noise
options.C: The generator sees the real images directly
options.D: Training is a min-max game between the two

questions.explanation: The generator only ever learns through the discriminator's verdict.

questions.question: You train the same model twice, once with batch size 32 and once with 512, keeping the learning rate fixed. The large-batch run learns noticeably slower per epoch. Why?

options.A: The loss function changes with batch size
options.B: It makes far fewer updates per epoch
options.C: Large batches cannot use momentum
options.D: Its gradient estimates are much noisier

questions.explanation: Fewer, better-aimed steps often lose to many rough ones, which is why the learning rate is usually scaled with the batch.

questions.question: Your network trains fine for 20 layers but the training error rises when you extend it to 50. What does that point to?

options.B: The deeper model is harder to optimise
options.C: The deeper model is overfitting
options.D: The batch size is now too small

questions.explanation: Overfitting would push training error down, not up. Skip connections are the standard answer here.

questions.question: You add batch normalisation and can suddenly train at ten times the old learning rate. What explains that?

options.A: The network now has fewer parameters
options.B: Dropout is no longer doing anything
options.C: Layer inputs now stay on a steady scale
options.D: The gradients have become much larger

questions.explanation: A big step in one layer no longer throws off everything stacked above it.

questions.question: You swap a 7x7 convolution for three stacked 3x3 ones and accuracy holds while parameters drop. Why does that work?

options.B: Small kernels always generalise better
options.C: The stride makes up the difference
options.D: The stack covers the same receptive field

questions.explanation: Three 3x3 layers see 7x7 between them, with fewer weights and two extra non-linearities.

questions.question: Your model hits 94% on validation but 71% once it meets real production images. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: The learning rate was set too low
options.B: The batch size was too small
options.C: Production data differs from your splits
options.D: The model has too few parameters

questions.explanation: Validation only tells you about the distribution it was drawn from.

questions.question: Your loss drops quickly then starts climbing after a few hundred steps. What would you change first?

options.B: Raise the learning rate
options.C: Lower the learning rate
options.D: Add several more layers

questions.explanation: A loss that falls then rises usually means the steps are too big to settle near a minimum.

questions.question: You add dropout and both training and validation accuracy get worse. What does that suggest?

options.A: The learning rate must be raised
options.B: Dropout was left on at inference
options.C: Dropout always costs accuracy
options.D: There was no overfitting to fix

questions.explanation: Regularisation costs capacity, which only pays off when there is a generalisation gap to close.

questions.question: You report 97% accuracy on a dataset where 97% of samples are negative. What should you do?

options.A: Accept it, the model has learned the task
options.B: Raise the classification threshold
options.C: Check precision and recall before believing it
options.D: Collect more data of both classes

questions.explanation: Predicting the majority class for everything gives exactly that number while detecting nothing.

questions.question: Your GAN produces sharp images but nearly all of them look alike. What has gone wrong?

options.B: Vanishing gradients
options.C: The discriminator has stopped learning
options.D: Overfitting to validation

questions.explanation: It found a few outputs that always fool the discriminator and stopped exploring.

questions.question: Your segmentation output places objects correctly but the boundaries come out blurred. What usually helps?

options.B: A larger batch size
options.C: Skip connections into the decoder
options.D: A smaller learning rate

questions.explanation: Downsampling throws away the fine detail that sharp edges need, and skips put it back.

questions.question: You strip every activation function out of a ten-layer network and accuracy falls to that of a linear model. Why?

options.B: The whole stack collapses to one linear map
options.C: The gradients vanish immediately
options.D: All the weights go to zero

questions.explanation: Composing affine maps gives another affine map, so ten layers express what one can.

questions.question: Two runs of the same network with different seeds give test scores 4 points apart. What is the right response?

options.A: Fix the seed and say nothing
options.B: Conclude the architecture is broken
options.C: Report whichever run scored highest
options.D: Report the mean and spread over several runs

questions.explanation: A single run tells you as much about the seed as about the model.

questions.question: You feed 64x64 images to a network trained on 32x32 that ends in a flatten and dense layer. It errors out. Why?

options.B: The pooling layers reject the size
options.C: The loss function needs a fixed size
options.D: The flatten produces a vector of the wrong length

questions.explanation: Convolutions cope fine. It is the dense layer after the flatten that expects one exact input length.

questions.question: You train with dropout but forget to switch to evaluation mode at test time. What do you observe?

options.B: The loss becomes negative
options.C: Accuracy improves slightly
options.D: The model refuses to run

questions.explanation: Dropout stays random, so the same image can get different answers each time you ask.

questions.question: You switch from SGD to Adam without changing the learning rate and training becomes unstable. What is the likely reason?

options.A: Adam requires a much larger batch size
options.B: Adam ignores the loss function
options.C: Adam cannot be used with mini-batches
options.D: Adam's per-parameter scaling makes steps effectively larger

questions.explanation: Adam usually wants a smaller base rate than SGD, commonly around 1e-3.

questions.question: You fine-tune a pretrained network on 300 images and it does worse than the frozen feature extractor. What would you try?

options.A: Train every layer for more epochs
options.B: Raise the learning rate across the whole model
options.C: Replace every layer with a fresh one
options.D: Freeze the early layers and train only the head

questions.explanation: 300 images is not enough to update generic early features without damaging them.

questions.question: Your recurrent model's loss occasionally spikes to a huge value mid-training. What usually fixes it?

options.B: Reducing the hidden size
options.C: Shuffling the time steps
options.D: Adding dropout to the recurrence

questions.explanation: Those spikes are exploding gradients, and clipping bounds the step they produce.

questions.question: Half the ReLU units in a layer output zero for every input in your dataset. What has happened?

options.A: Those units are stuck and no longer learning
options.B: The batch normalisation has failed
options.C: The layer is correctly sparse
options.D: The layer is overfitting

questions.explanation: A large step can push a unit somewhere its input is always negative, and no gradient returns to bring it back.

questions.question: Your autoencoder reconstructs the training images almost perfectly but its codes are useless for anything else. What is the likely problem?

options.A: The learning rate is too low
options.B: The bottleneck is too wide to force compression
options.C: The decoder has too few layers
options.D: The images are not normalised

questions.explanation: With enough room in the code, the network can simply copy its input across.

questions.question: Your model must accept images of varying size but still output 10 class scores. What change achieves that?

options.A: Resize every input before the first layer
options.B: Add a recurrent layer at the end
options.C: Replace the dense head with global average pooling
options.D: Increase the number of kernels

questions.explanation: Convolutions have no fixed input size, and global pooling collapses whatever spatial extent is left.

questions.question: Your model overfits and you suspect most of the 500 input features are noise. Which regulariser do you reach for?

options.B: L2, since it shrinks every weight a little
options.C: L1, since it zeroes irrelevant weights outright
options.D: Removing the bias terms

questions.explanation: L2 would shrink those weights but leave them contributing noise to every prediction.

questions.question: You must pick between two models: one with 88% train and 86% validation accuracy, another with 99% train and 81% validation. Which do you ship?

options.A: The second, since it fits the data better
options.B: The first, since it generalises better
options.C: Neither, both are unusable
options.D: Either, the difference is not meaningful

questions.explanation: Validation accuracy is the estimate of unseen performance, and the second model's gap shows clear overfitting.

questions.question: Your defect detector must not miss any faulty part, and a few false alarms are acceptable. Which metric matters most?

options.B: Training loss
options.C: Recall
options.D: Precision

questions.explanation: Recall is the share of real defects you catch. Precision would matter more if false alarms were the expensive error.

questions.question: You initialise a network's weights far too large and the activations saturate in the first forward pass. What follows?

options.B: The batch size must be increased
options.C: The loss becomes negative
options.D: Gradients are near zero and learning stalls

questions.explanation: Saturated units sit in the flat part of their activation, where the derivative is close to nothing.
